The 4th year student of bachelor program,  Group MM, Ayere   Teniola   Signature                      _____________________________________ INTRODUCTION Mergers and acquisitions (M&A) are critical components of corporate strategy. While mergers and acquisitions are frequently viewed as primarily financial transactions, they also involve an important "human side" (Sarala et al. 2019).   Companies benefit from M&A activities in a variety of ways, including increased cash flow, easier financing, experienced employees, quick customer acquisition, a mature operational and administrative system, reduced risk of business failure, reduced time to enter new businesses, reduced business risk, and so on (Hariyani, Serfianto and Yusticia, 2011). This practice can be conducted out both domestically and internationally, and it can involve a minority share part or a takeover of controlling management. Local buyers who buy in their own country have a better chance of succeeding than foreign buyers since they are more knowledgeable and confident about all aspects of the desired asset (Malone and Zicheng, 2008) .   This thriving market was characterized by an increasing number of dealmakers and deal options. Unlike the relatively simple deal market of 20 years ago, which consisted primarily of corporate buyers and some financial investor activity, today's M&A landscape includes significant participation not only from corporate buyers but also from add-on deals ( whereby  an investor purchases and incorporates various platform assets to create scale), financial investors, special purpose acquisition companies (SPACs), and venture capital (VC)/corporate venture capital (VC)/co (CVC) , David Harding, Andrei  Vorobyov , Suzanne Kumar, and Siobhan Galligan , 2022) . Figure  1 Global  M&A Deal Market Value  2000-2021  (In Trillions of US Dollars)  Source:  Bain  & Company( Deologic ) While the total deal value of strategic buyers (both corporate buyers and private equity portfolio add-ons) increased by 47 percent year on year in 2021, the value of these other types of M&A increased roughly twice as much.  ( David Harding, Andrei  Vorobyov , Suzanne Kumar, and Siobhan Galligan , 2022).  Moreover, d espite the fact that cross-border mergers  and acquisitions (M&As) are a common way for multinational corporations (MNCs) to enter foreign markets, the failure rate remains high (see, for example, Chen, Chang, and Lin 2010; meta-analyses by King, Dalton, Daily, and Covin 2004; Schlingemann & Moeller 2002). The integration period after the deal is done demonstrates how successfully synergies may be realized and how well both sides of the old firms can work together in harmony to realize synergy effects (Fried, Tiegs, Naughton, & Ashforth 1996). Integrating personnel to establish a new team is a key aspect of post-merger integration. The acquisition research yields inconsistent results on how to best integrate HR within the M&A process (Haspeslagh & Jemison 1991: 22–27; Weber & Tarba 2010). Material resources, such as computer centers, are easily transferable; yet, competitors can quickly copy them. As a result, good material resource integration will only yield a marginal increase that may only yield a short-term profit. Immaterial resources, such as staff integration or entire divisions of employees, are obviously more difficult to move and integrate successfully in order to capture value.   However, if this is achieved well, it creates competitive advantages that are difficult to duplicate (Barney 1991).     "International mega-mergers and acquisitions have grown rapidly in the last decade, with no signs of such activity slowing down" (Lakshman, 2011, p. 605).Even in the midst of recent turmoil in global financial markets, M&A activity has increased in the majority of sectors around the world. M&As remain a dominant growth strategy for many companies around the world. Many CEOs see several key components to justifying an M&A, such as cost savings, synergies, product expansion, distribution channel expansion, and achieving economies of scale (McDonald, Coulthard, & deLange, 2005) .  Many mergers and acquisitions fail owing to a lack of an effective   post-merger integration   ( PMI )   between the acquirer and the target companies, a difficulty that becomes much more challenging when companies cross national borders. The    PMI stage has been described as being critical in determining the overall success of M&As in this regard (Angwin & Meadows, 2015; Brueller, Carmeli, & Markman, 2016; Bauer & Matzler, 2014; Stahl et al., 2013).   At the very least, M&As threaten to upset the employees of the merging firms; people-related concerns and internal or contextual cultural mismatches have been extensively proposed to have an impact on PMI's success (Gomes et al., 2013; Stahl et al., 2013; Vuuren et al., 2010). To date, the majority of M&A-related research has focused on socio-cultural and psychological aspects to explain PMI (Gomes et al., 2013; Stahl et al., 2013). Gomes et al. (2013), for example, identified leadership, teamwork, communication, and cultural differences as important criteria for a successful PMI. According to Stahl et al. (2013), cultural and management style similarity, the pattern of dominance across merging firms, the acquirer's level of cultural tolerance, and the social context surrounding a takeover are all factors that influence M&A performance or underperformance. There are significant challenges when blending the human and technical components of two companies during the post-merger integration phase. Having a strong post-merger integration process can help organizations overcome any issues that arise during the merger's development and design. Epstein (2004) explained: A strong [post-merger integration] process can also overcome merger activity undertaken by CEOs for political reasons, to cement their legacy, or to achieve other personal rather than shareholder-related goals. (page 175).    A poor post-merger integration, on the other hand, can significantly and negatively impact the best-laid merger plans. Organizations far too often fail to take the necessary steps to develop an effective post-merger integration strategy (Epstein, 2004). The importance of leadership is also reflected in the integration team's post-merger integration process. For the integration team to be successful, the element of leadership must be demonstrated through the team's makeup and configuration. According to research, the post-merger integration leader  must be an outgoing, confident, and fully committed senior executive (Epstein, 2004). Without effective leadership, key areas such as communication can fail, jeopardizing the integration process's outcomes (Nemanich & Keller, 2006).   It's worth noting that the majority of employee perspective research on M&As is now focused on the acquire r  company. In this paper, we will look upon the perception of the employees from the acquire d  firm. Under what conditions, would acquiring firm employees see the new “combined” firm as inferior? Could the acquirers be seen as an inferior group and why? More studies from the acquiring firm employees’ perspective would give answers to such questions.  Perception is the process of interpreting and organizing sensations by organisms to produce a meaningful interpretation of reality.  Sensory  is  usually related to the immediate, physical aspects of reality. An unprocessed result of the stimulation of sensory receptors in the eyes, ears, and nose, skin, or tongue. On the other hand, perception describes the ultimate experience of the world and typically involves further processing of sensory information. Through the lens of perception, we gain knowledge about the properties and elements of the environment essential to our survival. However, perception not only shapes our experience of the world around us; it also provides us with the ability to act within our environment (Sternberg 1996).    Perception can be divided into two groups; bottom-up theories and top-down theories respectively. The top-down theorists approach perception from the top, focusing on expectations, prior knowledge, and other higher-level cognitive processes and then working downward from there to sensory data.  While bottom-up theorist ‟ s start from the   bottom and consider the perceived physical stimulation, the evident form or pattern, and also   work their way approximately higher-level cognitive procedures such as the organising concepts(Sternberg 1996).   According to Nahavandi and Malekzadeh (1993), what is required for the 1990s and beyond is to rediscover mergers as a strategic alliance and a partnership, rather than as a war. The loss of key individuals and the loss of organizational effectiveness, according to Bibler (1989), are two major human concerns that might occur during acquisitions. However, it was also stated in literature by Grove (1996) that companies often in most cases make purchases or acquire other companies as a diversionary tactic, something they undertake to avoid dealing with serious changes their organization is experiencing.  The acquired organization, according to Gussow (1978), is one that is consumed by the acquiring corporation and potentially destroyed.   In a  literature   from  Marks and  Mirvis  (1982 ), it  was ascertained  that for  months after the  acquisition , employees spent  a significant portion of their  workday  speculating about the  deal  (p.116).  According to Weiss (1989),  majority of the  resistance to change   behavio rs  in today’s workplace goes unnoticed  by employers,  basing this statement from r epotted cases of  83 percent  of the total respondents  in a  survey .  In his study, Kroeger and Thuesen (1992) identified mergers and acquisitions as one of the major trends and sources of stress in today's workplace. In a sense, a host of top scholars in the field of Mergers and Acquisitions have proven and  explained that   employee stress related problems tend to decrease productivity and increase absenteeism in the  workplace .  Moreover, there is  concerned that surprisingly little research has been done on how people cope with the aftermath of an acquisition  (Cooper (2000).  This study aims to contribute with new insights and knowledge on  the perception of employees on  Mergers and Acquisition  so as to   propose measures on how companies can deal with employee’s perceptions and how to make sure that these perceptions do not worsen the effectiveness of their work Research Object  Post-Merger Integration Processes Research  Subject Employee Perception Goal To identify the factors that affect employees’ perception of international mergers and integration  Objectives Conduct Literature Analysis Conduct Virtual Interviews from Employees of Selected Cases Collect and Analyse Data  Provide recommendations and measures for dealing with employee’s perception Research Questions In order to be able to analyse as well as process the data around  the problem discussion to archive the aim of this paper, The following question will be  explored. POST-MERGER INTEGRATION STAGE: LITERATURE REVIEW ANALYSIS Mergers  and  Acquisitions can be especially challenging to employees,  which in many case always    impact  their efficiency. Whether hugely different or sharing commonness, hardly ever do  two   cultures  conveniently and also smoothly combine into one.  Trustworthy and  knowledgeable staff members are the structure to any workforce.  Through the  times of unpredictability, staff members are most likely to become nonproductive or  tend to  leave. Past the direct  effects  of worker turn over, companies can shed   a significant amount money  in productivity and also minimized levels of customer service, with broader ramification to the bottom line business outcomes. Even more, when employees  who have serve years with understanding and experience    decide to leave,  they just can not be quickly replaced over night. Social culture adaptability  suffers when the workforce does not have confidence and also  stability .  When  firms  combine, there are usually circumstances of redundancy. In these cases,  it  can result in lay-offs, or might call for shifting functions  or daily routine  of your staff members. While  lay-offs  usually can not be prevented  in some cases ,   the idea of  decreasing uncertainty among staff members is  integral .  The  workers that are being laid off must be informed promptly and be supplied with severance bundles, when possible, as well as most importantly treated in a considerate fashion.  However, the workers that get to keep their jobs  ought to have clear guidelines on their role within the new company, and a development plan that will help them adjust to subsequent modifications.  It is well accepted that mergers and  acquisition  typically develop significant  stress  for employees and  managers  of both the  acquiring   and  acquired  firms that result in attitudinal and efficiency troubles in addition to  the  turnover of valued workers. Buono  and  Bowditch (1989) noted that adverse reactions may bring about significantly reduced levels of job fulfillment and also job protection and much less positive attitude in the direction of  the top management . Workers typically handle the unpredictability surrounding a  merger  by decreasing  their amount  of commitments as well as rather make use of the energy either to manage stress and anxiety and  well as  complication or look for brand-new employment opportunities. (Fulmer and Gilley,1998). I t's vital to note that the integration process employs a variety of integration methodologies. The PMI process in the merging business will be affected by this integration strategy and factors. Some researchers have taken approaches to the integration process. Nahavandi and Malekzadeh (1988) recognized distinct ways of acculturation based on two key variables from the perspective of the acquiring company: the degree of relatedness between the two companies and the purchasing company's tolerance for diversity. In an article,  Koch identifies three critical post-merger integration success factors: clear leadership, high standards, and a shared performance culture. Clear leadership ensures that management is always able to make clear fundamental decisions, such as B. personnel appointments or the definition of areas of responsibility. The second point raised by Koch is a high level of aspiration. On the one hand, this ensures that all synergy potentials are exhausted; on the other hand, the integration should be viewed as an opportunity to critically question existing corporate functions and business and, if necessary, restructure them. Developing and communicating a motivating vision and a growth strategy is also part of a high level of aspiration. According to Koch, the third success factor is a common performance culture. Koch sees cultural integration as an undervalued success factor that is frequently overlooked in favor of hard factors.   The consulting firm AT Kearney points  and added more factors to what Koch identified in his success factors fo r  PMI. AT Kearney points out  seven key factors of successful mergers, which they gained from their already mentioned Global PMI Survey in addition to practical observations. AT Kearney lists the first of the seven key factors of successful integration as having a  vision.  According to AT Kearney, cost synergies are often at the fore in mergers, so that the merger partners often lack the idea in the form of a trend-setting vision and strategies that can be derived from it. The second factor is  leadership  addresses the fact that the new, joint management of two merged companies is often nominated too late, which creates a  temporary management vacuum, which can lead to decision-making paralysis, management   power struggles and the drain of human resources.   Another success factor according to AT Kearney is growth. As already described under the vision aspect, cost synergies are often in the foreground, although growth should be the actual merger topic.  Fast wins, which a merged company achieves and, above all, communicates, serve to convince the employees of the success of the merger. Because by no means do all stakeholders, especially employees, see the need for a merger and therefore do not necessarily like working in the new company. To turn this negative attitude into a positive one, signals that show quick wins for those involved are important. Fast gains can be, for example, the reduction of bureaucracy, the improvement of the working environment or new supplier contracts or improved customer service.  AT Kearney sees the cultures a central success factor of successful integrations. According to AT Kearney, cultural barriers are often negated, which means that professional culture management that deals with the necessary changes to create and shape a new corporate culture is missed. As the sixth key factor, communication has the task of reducing frustration and insecurities among all stakeholders. The communication should not only work in one direction. The inclusion and thus co-determination of the communication partners also has a positive effect on those involved. A professional risk management the seventh success factor listed by AT Kearney. According to AT Kearney, the risks of the merger process, which can become threats,  barriers to success and ultimately catastrophes, are often only discussed theoretically but not managed professionally enough. After extensive  research  and collaborations,  Larsson  and Finkelstein (1999) developed and evaluated an integrative framework of M&A integration that merged both economic, ﬁnancial, organizational, strategic, and human resource aspects.    Figure  2   Larsson and Finkelstein (1999 ), Synergy Realization Approach They centered on three key factors to M&A success (determined as synergy realization): combination potential, achieving organizational integration, and managing employee resistance. Larsson and Finkelstein (1999) argued that combination potential represents opportunities to realize synergies. In other words, Combination potential was measured in terms of similarities and complementarities of the merging firms' marketing and manufacturing operations, rather than firm relatedness. The study's findings revealed that having the ability to combine assets improved M&A success.  This favorable correlation, according to Larsson and Finkelstein (1999), is due to the necessity to actively manage the integration process  in order to  maximize the synergy potential that already exists.   M&A success has also been found to be boosted by organizational integration. Employee resistance, on the other hand,  reduced  it. Because Larsson and Finkelstein (1999) discovered that similar management styles ,  reflecting commonalities between the merging organizations' cultures reduced employee resistance, they recommended that acquisition managers should pay attention to cultural elements after M&A. The article discussed the importance of an effective TMT during the post-acquisition integration process. It was shown that most acquisitions fail during this process as major clashes and conflicts occur. TMT’s play an important role in this process as they will be able to assimilate information, delegate tasks, use their capabilities to assess the situation and achieve the desired organizational goal. It was also argued that TMTs should possess capabilities that will allow them to prevent acquisition failure through successfully managing the integration process and enhancing organizational performance. Moreover, it was stated that these capabilities can be derived from two levels: first, the individual level, and second, from the total unit of the team. Finally, the paper also stressed the fact that the members of TMT should behave in the interests of firm and team performance to ensure the success of the acquisition. If the merger is to enjoy economies of scale, the authors expect turnover, unless the merged firm subsequently grows. In these results, workers with high wage residuals are more likely to exit the firm. Noooderhaven , (2016) ,  Occupational identification  is an important, yet overlooked, determinant in  post-merger integration .  In  their  article,  name   “ The Role of Occupational Identification ”. The  authors study focuses on the human aspect of post-merger integration, and in particular the role of occupational identification. The authors aim to go beyond much previous research in this line of research, which often has been one sided in its focus on employee resistance (Ford, Ford, &  D’Amelio ;  Piderit , 2000 ).The  authors describe how  they  test the hypotheses using data from a large-scale study of two merged service organizations. The merger was characterized by both a high degree of integration and a high degree of autonomy. This type of integration process is labeled “symbiotic”. A symbiotic  post-merger integration  process constitutes an ideal setting for the research. Because the two companies were not quickly and fully integrated, but gradually, and with different speeds and intensity for different departments and functions, the merger confronted different employees and occupational groups with divergent conditions, necessary for testing Hypotheses 3 and 4. The gradual integration process provided them with the opportunity to observe how the effect of occupational identification evolved over time, as the integration became more intense (Hypothesis 2). In the results, both occupational identification and willingness to cooperate have a relatively high mean, 3.85 and 4.18, respectively. Moreover, age and managerial status in the sample have a positive impact on the extent to which people are willing to cooperate in the integration process, and professional status a negative impact. The latter finding is contrary to the expectations.  However,  as the authors went through the interview transcripts, the authors first focused on generating categories of targets for identification in a  post-merger integration  context. This process confirmed that, next to the organization, occupation is an important identification target category for organization members.   Finally, drawing on quantitative and qualitative data from a field study of a merger between two service-sector companies, the authors establish that  occupational identification  positively influences employees’ willingness to cooperate in the  post-merger integration  process. This positive influence is stronger for uniformed  personnel and  tends to become weaker later in the integration process.  Building on previous studies emphasizing the importance of various types of identification (e.g.,  Ashforth  et al, 2008 ), the authors argue that  occupational identification  is an important, yet overlooked, determinant in  post-merger integration .   To the best of the knowledge, the effects of occupational identification have not yet been studied in a merger context.   In recent time,  Authors differ in various PMI approaches for IS integration depending on the type of the merger: absorption, best-of-breed, co-existence, renewal process/new-build ( Toppenberg  and Henningsson, 2014; Eckert et al, 2012) or capacity pooling, capacity consolidation, one-stop-shop, focused intervention, new business model (Chatterjee and  Brueller , 2015) or overhaul, symbiosis, absorption, preservation ( Brunetto , 2006) or renewal, standardization, takeover, synchronization, and best-of-breed ( Wijnhoven  et al, 2006). However,  Due to the fact that  business process management  (BPM) plays a vital role in organizational changes (Rohloff, 2011;  Jochem  et al, 2011), a key element in post-merger integration ( PMI ) is the process harmonization.   In the paper, the author described  the search processes and  explained the approach to identify process harmonization after  M&A , using the strategy developed ( Kitchenham  and Charters, 2007). As well as A three-stage approach  that was  executed and documented according to  Brocke  et al (2009), covering the requirements for search documentation of  Kitchenham  and Charters (2007) as well.  The paper findings shows  that process  harmonization  and the need to unify  management system s after an  M&A  are mentioned in many of the  PMI  articles, so the necessity is well known, but not deepened. 47 percent of the analysed articles are miscellaneous, which in other words means the articles appeared in the search hits, but in a different context.  In the environment of PMI, BPM and change management, there is a huge amount of research articles covering multiple single particles needed for process harmonization. Due to the high failing rate of M&As, the literature provides numerous success factors for PMI .  The biggest examples and notable cases of M& As  comes from the acquisitions of  start-ups . In a sense,  start-ups  being acquired is a sign of success and achievement. In a recent study of  the  “ Acquisitions of Start-ups by Incumbents ” by  Nir N.  Brueller  and Laurence Capron (2021).  Seven start-ups’ firms were studied,   two of them (Platinum and Actinium) exhibited close collaboration with the incumbent, three exhibited moderate interaction with incumbents (Palladium, Osmium, and Silver), and two had little or no interaction with the incumbent (Erbium and Lutetium).   All the sampled start-ups were located in the larger Tel Aviv metropolitan area.   The authors' study relies on the “triangulation” of multiple data sources and types: publicly available data, internal archives obtained locally from the companies in the sample, interview data, and follow-up interviews with the multinational acquirers’ executives both locally and at the acquirers’ headquarters.   The authors' findings provide support to the crucial role of  start-up’s embeddedness in spotting entrepreneurial opportunities through increased inform ation gained by interactions with incumbents at an early stage. Initial embeddedness   which varied widely across the seven start-ups   affected start-ups’ ability to learn how the incumbent viewed the focal opportunity. Such information advantage in turn enables the start-ups to initiate a more informed opportunity-spotting process. The authors' findings also indicate that   direct technological insights aside   prior social embeddedness may facilitate continued close incumbent-start-ups interaction and may yield strategic feedback from the former regarding the business aspects of any opportunities being pursued by the latter. Understanding how the start-ups is positioned vis-à-vis the relevant incumbent increases potential investors’ clarity on the chances of a successful In conclusion, opening the “black box” of start-ups’ collaboration with incumbents prior to their acquisition sheds light on some of the critical stages of the entrepreneurial process. Figure  4   Gregory's  Top-Down Approach  Theory  (1990) Psychologist Richard Gregory argues that processing is a  top-down  process. Since we do not see simple  configurations,  but we see complex objects, for this to be possible, an active search for the best possible interpretation of the available characteristics is necessary. According to Gregory, this interpretation, defined as «hypothesis control», can only take place according to a  top-down  approach thanks to which we «build» our perceptions through our cognitive processes  ( Gregory 1990 ) .   This  approach correlates  with  findings of  Lundqvist (2012), who concluded that  to  get quick outcomes in a merger, top-down strategies are essential. According to Gregory, therefore, it is our experiences and knowledge about a stimulus, stored in memory, that help us make inferences. That is, we create a perceptual hypothesis on the perceived stimulus, based on its memory and on past experiences related to it.  For Gregory, therefore, perception consists in making the best guess about what we are seeing. In terms of visual perception, Gregory argues that about 90% of visual information is lost by the time it arrives at the brain for processing. In visual illusions, such as the Necker  cube, according to Gregory, the brain creates erroneous assumptions, making several perceptual errors. Bottom - Up  Approach (Gibson Theory) Also  K nown as  T he Ecological Approach  In the  bottom-up  processing approach, perception starts from sensory input, from the stimulus. Therefore, perception can be described as based on data. For example, there is a flower in the center of a person's field. The sight of the flower and all information about the stimulus are transported from the retina to the visual cortex in the brain. The signal travels in one direction. According to Gibson, each stimulus has sensory information specific enough to make it possible to recognize it without the intervention of higher cognitive processes (ecological theory). Perception is not subject to  hypotheses but  is rather a direct phenomenon:  “What you see is what you get” . Our environment, according to Gibson  (1966) , can provide sufficient details about the stimulus ( e.g.,  size, shape, distance, etc.), and the perception of the stimulus may not depend on prior knowledge or  experience  of the stimulus. Cognitive processes such as memory to access  experience  would therefore not be necessary to recognize the stimulus, which would already have its own "internal order" which would allow direct perception. The internal order, constituted by the spatial and temporal distribution of the stimulus, allows a direct "availability" to its recognition. Gibson called this availability of the stimulus "affordance". Affordance would be what allows the observer to extract the characteristics that define the use and purpose of the perceived object. Still according to the theory of direct perception, the affordance suggested by the object to the observer is based not only on the physical factors possessed by the object, but also on the psychological and physiological state of the observer.  Figure  5   Bottom-Up Approach Gibson Theory  (1996) One of the criticisms leveled at Gibson's theory of direct perception is that which refers to optical illusions, which would demonstrate that the characteristics of the stimulus alone do not allow its correct perception. Gibson responded by arguing that the stimuli Gregory  refers to in optical illusions are artificial images, not images that can be found in a person's normal visual environment.  Nasser’s  P er ceptual  C ycle  Another theory of perception is that of analysis by synthesis formulated by Neisser (1976) which is halfway between the direct theory of Gibson and the constructivist theory proposed by Gregory. Neisser reconciles the two extreme positions bottom up (data-driven processing) and top down (knowledge-driven processing). The data in memory (beliefs / expectations, anticipatory patterns) guide our exploration activity ( top down ), and the new data acquired from the exploration in turn modify our expectations and beliefs ( bottom up ). For Neisser, perceiving is not the same as assigning an object to a category, but constructing patterns suitable for various situations.  These schemes may undergo continuous changes in relation to new information coming from the environment. This mechanism has adaptive functions. In this perceptual cycle, the dimensions of movement and time are of great importance. With the movement of the subject there are continuous changes in the optical arrangement of objects and this makes the reality that is being observed clearer. Time, on the other hand, is fundamental, because it takes time to perceive. Research  Methodology   This chapter describes the research methodology and explains what kind of research was conducted in this study. It also specifies the study's demographic, as well as the sample strategies employed, data collection methods, and data processing processes. The subsections that make up this chapter are as follows: Design of the study, sample size, data collection, and data analysis Qualitative Study Qualitative research methods allow for in-depth research on a topic (Bryman & Bell, 2013),  which  is consistent with our research goals. Since this paper aims to  delve deeply  into the perception of employees in the three cases indepthly and to  conduct  a comparative study, a qualitative  method  is the best approach  for accomplishing  this  goal . In contrast to the  quantitative method or approach which has  various  ways of collecting and interpreting data.  While  qualitative research methods focus on subjective interpretation of data, quantitative methods seek to get a broad overview of the research topic. Since the focus of this paper is to  delve deep into  the perception of employees, it will  undoubtedly   be   difficult to  employ  quantitative methods due to measurement difficulties.  In some ways , it will be hard to measure perception based on quantitative data based on some predefined variables. This can definitely be misleading as information and results that would be  difficult  to describe. Therefore, for this paper, qualitative research is the most appropriate approach because statements such as perception can be understood in greater depth. The  idea of perception  is found in what is said and expressed rather than quantifiable. That is the reason a qualitative approach is the  best suited  for this paper.   The flexibility that a qualitative approach enables for is one of its primary advantages (Saunders et al., 2009). It allows for the organization of information based on  variation , linkages, and relationships. In addition, if opposing viewpoints or nuances are discovered during data collecting, the research problems can be adjusted.  However, how you collect  your  data is integral,  For  example,  considering and fairly weighting all of the responses supplied, is critical for a qualitative research approach. As a result, in order to deliver a credible result, the research method must be characterized by openness and perceptiveness. 2.1.1 Cases  Selection In this paper  I  chose to look at three separate acquisitions , which  are:   Citrix acquisition of  Wrike Yandex acquisition of Wind and Playtika’s acquisition of Reworks To minimize the issues associated with performing in-depth research across different industries (Schweizer, 2005), we limited our cases to those within a single industry (Eisenhardt, 1989).   The research for the  bachelor’s  thesis began with a review of numerous well-known M&A case studies. The rationale for well-known cases is the ease with which substantial data may be accessed in order to conduct a meaningful analysis.  For a variety of reasons, the IT industry provides an outstanding research environment. To begin with, the information technology industry has demonstrated a number of new development patterns, including a high level of integration. At the same time, it demonstrates the worldwide context in which the global high-tech industry is on a sound growth path. Acquisitions can be made for a wide range of strategic reasons in this environment, enhancing the odds of a good fit at each level of integration. Furthermore, due to technological obstacles, acquirers frequently pay a bigger premium for the target company than in many other industries. The selection of cases in the information technology industry for this study was influenced by a number of criteria. First and foremost, I  sort out to consider how   essential it was that the case organizations  picked  had recently been acquired. We could acquire less accurate and biased representations of earlier perspectives from the respondents if we looked at less recent acquisitions. Second ,  the case organizations were similar in that  all the cases  operated in the European market, the target firms had no prior acquisition history, and  the cases represented in this paper  were  acquired  quite recently. Third ,  to cover all integral parts,  US/European-based   acquiring companies were chosen  to reduce the potential for false impacts of national culture (Lee et al., 2015). PROPOSITIONS As a result of the analysis I  have  develop ed the  following propositions : Preposition 1,  If the integration team is perceived as negative ly, then all other aspect of the integration will be affected and perceived negatively  Preposition  2,  If training staff as a measure is introduced in the early stages ,  then  integration aspect such as communication and  strategy   will  be   positively or  neutrally perceived by the employees Preposition  3,  Employees attitude towards change becomes more positive if communication is perceived as consistent by employees 2.2. Discussion of Results In the following section the discussion of the analysed cases will be presented.  This discussions will be  based on the empirical analysis provided in the prior chapter, and a comparison with the expected pattern stated in the theoretical part will be made. Employee Perception Of Measures All the  three perceived the measures and systems implemented by the integration team differently.  Among other things, the participants reflected on their perception of the  degree of integration among other things. They expressed their impressions on the integration team, the communication, strategy and speed which was visualised in table 2.  Firstly, there were cases were the respondents pointed out the communication at each level of the integration, the transformation integration of systems , and the problems and uncertainties that come with them.   Epstein (2005) addresses the importance of an integration team that works with the integration programming earlier in the process. According to the respondents, they all agree that there is actually an integration team that endured that communication was achieved throughout the organisation. Most of the low level staff stated that they did not receive information of the integration until the later stages of the acquisition process  which made them not feel undervalued by the team . In fact, some respondents claim to have heard about the acquisition officially through the press release. They claim there was a couple of months with no news and nothing happening. The challenges and uncertainties sprang in part from the fact that the acquired firms of the integration process did not have enough time to complete the procedure realistically and on schedule.   Which explains one of the propositions that was derived from the empirical study cross cases analysis which argues that  If the integration team is perceived as negatively, then all other aspect of the integration will be affected and perceived negatively or neutral . This was the reality, as  a result  of that  it was tough to execute for case 3.  However, the planning process could have been better if it had been strategic, but it did not work out in actuality. It was made too soon and with not enough information. Employee Perception Of Integration  For long-term value generation and competitive advantage, the strategic vision and how the organization fits together are crucial.  If a company wants to integrate in order to obtain talents and expertise, it is critical to provide benefits by matching them with the appropriate abilities, people, and understanding of the activities that are most in demand.  In this context, the case 2 acquisition matches that description, the acquired expertise, which the participants also remark  in the empirical studies as well as the market acquiring company will potentially reach.  One of the respondents in case 1 stated that the merger was a fantastic experience, but that the journey was far from being finished because they still had a lot of work to do, such as blending their cultures, procedures, tools, and systems. However, in my opinion blending cultures does not imply that everything will operate flawlessly from the start, but the strategy is to be executed during the integration process, as the time will  not just be  limited to only a few months  or even   a  year.  According to  Epstein  (2005), Studies must be conducted, as well as a thorough evaluation, in order to choose the best system suitable for  company’s  future.  Epstein (2005) emphasizes the importance of a thorough examination of both companies' assets long before the merger.  This is in order to acquire information on financial information, how corporate cultures work together, technology capability, and staff competencies.  Moreover, talking about the communication, majority of the information is kept hidden, it was also mentioned by a handful of respondents that the information was announced in the later stages.  In my opinion in all  the  cases,  there was a concentration on the most vital aspects, while others were overlooked.  In recent times there are a lot of changes happening and these changes are often fast to talk about a few (Lewis, Romanaggi & Chapple, 2010). Which agrees with the empirical findings from all three cases. He further stressed that organizations must learn to be able to effect change in such a situation, which means that firm employees must be able to be led in new directions and operate in new methods .  A respondent in case 3 claimed the acquisition was not communicated to all the employees, only a significant subset.  Most employees got to know after the deal had been finalised . (2000), because it has a very significant effect on employees, as well as being important for their adoption of a new culture and desire to change . According to Epstein (2004), communication is not only vital for employees, but it is also important for clients in order to eliminate uncertainty, misunderstanding, and frustration.  There are several parallels between the literature and how the integration process unfolded in Case 1. This was also one case 1 respondent's perspective, who went on to claim that the acquisition had been a success.  Which also in a sense explains proposition 2 which illustrates that  “ If training staff as a measure is introduced in the early stages, then integration aspect such as communication and strategy will  be positively  perceived by the employees ” . Employee Perception As previously stated, the participants had a wide range of experiences in terms of integration.  For a successful change process, the involvement  of employees is critical,  for example, by allowing them to participate in decision-making as well as to come up with a proposal to build a sense of trust. It's a good idea to have a rewards program in place to keep people motivated and productive. Most significant, however, is the ability to convey the company's direction to employees, as well as give assistance and mentoring (Lewis, Romanaggi, & Chapple   2010) . Change Resistance is natural to humans.   The perceptions that came out of the interviews were unique for a variety of reasons, in my opinion. Some of the respondents were involved in some different part of the integration as managers and team  leaders . CHAPTER 3: CONCLUSIONS   Below are the results of the answer     to  the research question that were developed from the problem discussion. Theoretical contributions are highlighted,  followed by the practical implications for managers.  The research question examined was : How  do  employees perceive different organizational measures during post-merger integration stage? The post-merger integration process was perceived  by  employees   in different ways and was met with different reactions by various  employees . Their impressions of the  integration  were  not just on the of integration process itself but also on other factors such as their perception towards the company strategy, change and  pace of the integration.  The participants' differing perceptions of the post-merger integration can be attributed to a number of factors. Firstly,  In the cases analyse,  we can see  that the perceptions of the respondents differ depending on their role or in the organisation. More senior staff due to the fact that they knew about the acquisition all along had different perception about the integration. However, this was different for the low-level staff whose career were mostly on the line due to the consequences an M&A can bring. Another  reason  the perception was met with different reactions was the measures that was put in place by different acquiring companies. For  example,  in case 1 majority of the respondents perceived the integration process more positive due to the early training measures that was put in place by the leaders in the integration  team to  mitigate uncertainties and  ambiguities  an d  to better help the employees assimilate and adjust to the  apparent change.  In their case, the negative perception was minimised by taking proactive steps so as to give the employees some sense of inclusion.  In the analysis  there  were   evidence of differences in the  type of measures implemented by the companies during the post-merger integration stage. Moreover, a  lot depended on where the companies are in the process of integration .  Several respondents stated that system and process adjustments were still essential, but that they needed to be improved.   In addition, majority of the respondents stated that the implementation was quick in terms of the speed. This was expressed in all the cases giving the fact that all acquisitions were completed in the year 2021.  At the same time, it is critical for businesses to act quickly and not get stuck in the past, which means the integration still has to be done quickly and efficiently. Another  integral  perception that was observed by the employees during the empirical studies was communication. A host of the respondents didn’t feel included in the early stages which brought about distrust in the company’s strategy in some cases. For example, in case  2 an  employee practically disagrees with the  company’s  strategy starting the company  doesn’t really care about their employees but are only concern with squeezing out profit. Lack of early and consistent message communication between companies and their staff  during the integration stage  breeds the perception of uncertainty among  employees and this was also correlates with the results shown on the cross—case analysis table. It is critical to communicate strategy, goals, milestones, and expectations to stakeholders, including customers and employees. The general perception of the employees was that the work was not yet finished, but that it will be completed in the long run. The measures that will be employed in the new companies are still has yet to be determined. After studying the perception of employees in mergers and acquisition in all three cases it was discovered that they were several under-looked factors that leaders paid less attention to  during in the post-merger integration process. However, for   top management staff  leaders   there  is  a wide variety of articles and literature that have explained detailed textbook steps and important factors to   consider  during the post-merger integration process?   But there have been little or no literature on the IT industry specific factors that must be considered. After the empirical studies and analysis of the three cases in this  paper, after  studying the cases I have concluded that  three  factors  have come up  as  inte gral for leaders involved  in the post-merger integration process . The most significant factor being  truthful communication and transparency , humans not just employees are stressed by what we don’t know and uncertainties as they always assume the worst due to the lack of choice .   This is consistent with the literature, as one of the most frequently cited best practices for M&As is communication (Kay & Shelton, 2000; Larsson & Finklestein, 1999; Schweiger et al., 1987; Seo & Hill, 2005).  Many companies try to maintain mergers hidden from everyone until they are completed, after which the employees get wind of it.  This was seen in case 2 and 3 which ultimately had an effect on how the employees perceived change.  As a leader, it's tempting to keep the merger discussions private because you don't want anyone to consider leaving or anything else to go wrong before the deal is finalized.  Leaders  also don't want word of the merger to get out, and trusting your team requires a lot of confide nce  in yourself and in your team.   However, trusting your team and being open and honest is almost always a better long-term strategy.  Case 1 demonstrated to us that employees will  only trust you if you trust them, so  leaders  must take this into consideration  and take the first step.    Another factor  that was stressed by the respondents  was reassurance, a respondent told me he felt he had to put in double the work just to be sure he proves his competence to his manager during the integration process due to his stress of layoffs. He further went on to say this led to him burning out. Details are extremely important,  assuring them that their jobs are not at risk and being honest about it will go a long way towards making the integration process go more smoothly. Another respondent about the employee stock program. If they are going to change or remain the same. Reassurances that they are honest are just as effective as  increasing compensations of employees as said by a respondent. Employees wan reassurance on everything. However, it is also a good practice not to overpromise and under deliver with it comes to reassurance . Finally, a nother major factor to consider is the quality of plan . Naturally, everything begins with a detailed plan of action. Then the organization decides how the target will be implemented, and this must be done in accordance with a comprehensive plan supported by compelling logic.  As seen the case, there are plans and measures used which affected how employees perceived the integration. For this factor the quality of implementation is also very critical, it is very advisable to follow the plan cohesively  and  communicat e  effective ly to avoid confusion.   3.2.  Theoretical  Contribution This study contributes  to theory by  exploring the factors that will help explain the perception of employees  in international  mergers and acquisition s  in the field of information tec hnolog y.  Furthermore, the current study incorporates two theoretical approaches.  ( i.e   Epstein  (2004)  key  success factors for a successful merger and Larsson and Finkelstein (1999) , synergy realization theories). In doing so, it extends previous studies that relied almost entirely on the Epstein (2004) key success factors perspective to understand  how employees perceive  mergers and acquisition .   Secondly, in the empirical section, this thesis also contributes by giving details in explaining the relationship between communication and employee change  resistance.  In  the cross-cases analysis, the relationship between communication and employee change resistance was examined in all three cases, the findings  showed us that  consistent  communication positively changes  employee’s  perce ption of  change. This as however shown some difference with previous findings of  (Eby et al, 2000; Martin, 1998; Kotter, 1996) in the studied literature that  suggested that positive employee workplace attitudes are often critical in achieving organizational goals and in succeeding in change programmes.  The research also contributed to a better understanding and knowledge of how the integration process was perceived by employees in all IT three cases. 3.3.  Practical  Implications   In order to improve the factors that positively affect  employee’s  perception of mergers and acquisition. Some practical implications  have  been identified from this study.   Following the acquisition, a detained and consistent information of the integration plan must be communicated to the staff.   A good way of working during the integration was represented by good communication to all stakeholders  and  also a good collaboration between colleagues was mentioned by the respondents  The plan should not only focus on the company’s top executives, but it should give the  low-level  employees a perception of inclusion to collaborate  In order to reduce  employee,  burn out an d  lack of motivation,  employee reassurance strategies need to be assessed. The particular strategies leaders choose in order to account for the different employee needs across the phases of the post-merger integration  should be accessed since the lack of such can lead to integration  problems.  Furthermore ,  in  order to ensure trust between the merging  entitles ,  confidence in the integration team needs to be established.  Leaders   should ensure that their  aims and expectations help to build trust in the company. This  does  not only  help  to carry out the work well, but it also demonstrated the organization's appreciation for the employees.   It was also demonstrated in this study that  employee’s negative perception of the integration team causes  all other aspect of the integration  to be perceived negatively.  Companies  should ensure that  committed senior executives  who are involved in the integration process completely  disavows any biases from  their  previous  employer and that the team is  structured  away from the core business units in a  project management approach  to  aid effectiveness . Finally, c ultural integration can be difficult for merging parties when they either fail to recognize each other's cultures or take insufficient steps to allow their cultures to complement one another. It is critical to implement measures that aid in employee education. Companies can devote adequate attention to culture by connecting the cultural program to measurable and quantifiable business results.
